The graphic illustrates the nutritional consistency of two different types of dinners, medium baked potato and macaroni.
Both dinners are more constituents of carbohydrates, coloured in green,with respectively the 35% in the baked potato and 52% in the macaroni.
In the first dinner, the protein, in purple, occupie the second place in the pie with the 25%, followed by the glucose, in yellow, that represents just the 15% of the pie .
In the macaroni, the glucose and the protein occupies exactly the same amount of the pie with the 11% while the second place is occupied by the saturated fat, in dark blue, with the 21%, that, in contrast, it's only the 10% in the medium baked potato. The last 15% of the pie of the first dinner is completed by other nutrients, in light blue, while, in the second dinner, the other nutrients occupie just the 5% of the pie graph.

The pie charts compare the nutritional composition of two meals: a grilled chicken sandwich and a bowl of rice with vegetables.
In both meals, carbohydrates represent the largest proportion of the nutrients. They account for 40% of the rice and vegetable meal and 30% of the grilled chicken sandwich.
For the grilled chicken sandwich, protein makes up 28% of the total nutritional content, making it the second-largest component. Saturated fat accounts for 18%, while glucose represents 12%. The remaining 12% consists of other nutrients.
In contrast, the rice and vegetable meal contains a much lower proportion of protein, at 15%. Saturated fat is responsible for 10% of the total, whereas glucose makes up 20%. Other nutrients account for the remaining 15% of the meal.
Overall, carbohydrates are the dominant nutrient in both meals, although the grilled chicken sandwich contains more protein and saturated fat, while the rice and vegetable meal has a higher percentage of glucose and other nutrients.

The charts show the nutritional breakdown of two different meals: a grilled chicken sandwich and a serving of rice with vegetables.
Carbohydrates form the largest share in both meals. They account for 30% of the sandwich and an even larger 40% of the rice and vegetable dish.
Looking first at the grilled chicken sandwich, protein is the second most significant nutrient, making up 28% of the total. Saturated fat contributes 18%, while glucose represents 12%. The remaining 12% is made up of other nutrients.
The pattern is slightly different for the rice and vegetable meal. Protein accounts for only 15%, whereas glucose reaches 20%. Saturated fat is relatively low at 10%, and the final 15% consists of other nutrients.
In general, both meals are rich in carbohydrates, but the sandwich provides a greater proportion of protein and fat, while the rice-based meal contains more glucose and a slightly larger share of other nutrients.

Life is full of competition. Soon after the release from competitive university exams, the hard fighting for higher positions in a company will be waiting for you. While the competitiveness has been bringing us a convenient society, no small number of employments have been tired of never-ending competitions. No doubt about a positive effect on societies, but when we start to argue about the effect on individuals, it becomes more controversial. This essay will argue the drawbacks of competitiveness for individuals, especially from the perspective of Japanese modern society and typical Japanese office workers. I believe that cooperativeness and uniqueness are sometimes more important attitude than competitiveness.
Firstly, competitiveness causes less diversity. If there is a competition, there must be the same evaluation metrics to differentiate winners and losers. The evaluation procedure needs to limit the metrics into specific aspects of the human being. Therefore, it is extremely difficult to prove that the metrics are proper for all of the possible cases and effective for the HR evaluation. For example, Japanese companies are likely to judge quantitatively based on the numbers related to performance. However, how can we evaluate the cooperativity or the non-quantitative performance like in creative jobs? Most Japanese companies adopted the HR evaluation methods based on average working hours per day and number of reports monthly. That’s why the Japanese tend to overwork to fulfill this requirement. In addition, as a result of placing more importance in quantity of reports, rather than quality, Japanese employees are prone to choose easy and short-term tasks or outsourcing instead of building by their own methods. This tendency causes fewer complicated and long-term tasks.
Secondly, competitiveness causes less cooperativeness. Fewer people volunteer to play a disadvantageous role for smooth teamwork. Moreover, some people play an evil role by blaming or cheating the competitors in order to be superior to them. As a consequence, overall efficiency will decline and some people will be overestimated. Even if there are no problems temporarily, the long-lasting large companies will eventually become corrupt, and those who have the most political power, rather than real power, will remain at the top.
Consequently, competitiveness is the basis of capitalism which brings us a brighter and more convenient society, however, biased evaluation and less cooperative working environment possibly drive people into nervous breakdowns. Karoshi, overwork death derived from too much stress and less sleeping, is one of the most serious social problems in Japan. We may need to rethink about the competitiveness.

Modern society is highly competitive. From school examinations to career advancement, individuals are constantly encouraged to outperform others. Competition has undoubtedly contributed to economic growth and technological progress. However, while it may benefit society as a whole, its impact on individuals is often less positive. This essay argues that excessive competitiveness can be harmful because it reduces creativity and weakens cooperation among people.
To begin with, a highly competitive environment may discourage creativity and individual differences. In most competitions, success is measured using a limited set of criteria, which means people are rewarded for meeting specific standards rather than exploring new approaches. As a result, individuals tend to follow established methods instead of taking risks. For example, many organizations evaluate employees mainly according to measurable outcomes such as sales figures or productivity targets. Although these indicators are useful, they may overlook qualities such as innovation, leadership, and artistic talent. Consequently, workers may focus only on activities that improve their evaluation scores, reducing diversity in ideas and approaches.
Another disadvantage of competitiveness is that it can damage teamwork. When individuals are primarily concerned with their own success, they may become less willing to assist colleagues or share knowledge. In some cases, competition may even encourage unhealthy behaviors such as withholding information or taking credit for others' work. This can create a stressful atmosphere and reduce trust among team members. Since many tasks in modern organizations require collaboration, a lack of cooperation can ultimately lower overall productivity and effectiveness.
In conclusion, competition plays an important role in driving economic development and motivating people to improve themselves. Nevertheless, excessive competitiveness can limit creativity and undermine cooperation. Therefore, societies and organizations should seek a balance between competition and collaboration in order to promote both individual well-being and long-term success.

Competition is a common feature of modern life. People compete for places at universities, for jobs, and later for promotions in the workplace. There is little doubt that competition has helped societies become more prosperous and efficient. Yet its effects on individuals are not always beneficial. In my view, placing too much emphasis on competition can create problems, particularly by discouraging originality and weakening the spirit of cooperation.
One issue is that competition often rewards people according to a narrow set of standards. To compare individuals fairly, organizations usually rely on measurable criteria, but these criteria cannot capture every valuable quality. Employees may therefore focus on improving the numbers that matter for evaluations rather than developing new ideas or skills. For instance, workers whose performance is judged mainly by sales targets or output levels may avoid experimenting with unfamiliar methods because failure could damage their rankings. Over time, this can lead to a workplace where conformity is valued more highly than creativity.
Competition can also affect the way people interact with one another. In a strongly competitive environment, helping colleagues may be seen as less important than advancing one's own position. Some individuals become reluctant to share information or resources, especially when rewards are limited. Although this behavior may benefit certain people in the short term, it can harm the organization as a whole by reducing trust and teamwork. Successful businesses often depend on employees working together effectively, something that becomes more difficult when everyone is focused solely on personal achievement.
To sum up, competition has clear advantages and can encourage people to work harder. However, if it becomes excessive, it may reduce creativity and make cooperation more difficult. For this reason, a healthy balance between competitive and collaborative values is likely to produce better outcomes for both individuals and society.

Sugar-based drinks has become a popular beverage amongst the people especially in today's generation, the millenials. This essay will outline possible reasons on why people are consuming such drinks and also some ways for them to consume less.
To start, sugar is the main component of this beverage. Study shows that consuming this can boost a person's energy levels. Hence athelete ae addicted to this refreshments such as energy drinks. Furthermore, this can increase mental alertness especially to working individuals. For instance, not all workers has the capability to maintain their energy at work, sometimes they tend to get sleepy leading them to buy sugary drinks like milk teas. Not only that, advertisement can also be a factor. For example, young people gets curious whenever new sugared drinks is advertised especially if this is shown by their favorite icon. Lastly, consumer's lack of knowledge and misinformation regarding their product as evident by the coca cola lawsuit.
Although it may be difficut for people to stay away from this. There are ways that can help them drink less. One solution is for government to impose a price hike. Meaning high price can discourage them to buy this drinks. Second, just like alcohol, buying sugar-based drinks at malls or grocery store should have time limit. For instance, whoever will buy and sell beyond the allotted time should pay a fine. Also, Educating the public about the health issue of drinking too much sugar-based drinks should be prioritize. Finally, government should encourage company such as pepsiCo, and the Coca-cola company to be transparent of their nutritional facts.
In conclusion, sugar-based drinks has become popular due to advertisements and society's thinking that it can replenish their energy levels. In contrast, goverment should take the first step educate and encourage individuals to mimimize drinking this.

Sugar-sweetened beverages have become increasingly popular among people of all ages, particularly young adults. This essay will discuss several reasons why these drinks are widely consumed and suggest some measures that could help reduce their consumption.
One reason people drink sugary beverages is that they provide a quick source of energy. Many students and workers consume energy drinks, soft drinks, and sweetened coffee beverages to stay active throughout the day. In addition, these drinks are often associated with enjoyment and convenience. They are readily available in stores, restaurants, and vending machines, making them an easy choice for consumers. Advertising also plays a significant role in encouraging consumption. Beverage companies frequently use celebrities, athletes, and social media influencers to promote their products, which can attract young consumers. Another factor is the lack of awareness about the health risks associated with excessive sugar intake. Many people underestimate the connection between sugary drinks and conditions such as obesity, diabetes, and tooth decay.
Despite their popularity, there are several ways to encourage people to consume fewer sugar-based drinks. One effective measure is to increase taxes on these products, making them more expensive and less attractive to buyers. Governments can also require clearer nutritional labeling so that consumers better understand what they are drinking. In addition, public health campaigns could educate people about the negative effects of excessive sugar consumption and encourage healthier alternatives such as water, milk, or unsweetened beverages. Schools and workplaces could also play a role by limiting the availability of sugary drinks and promoting healthier options.
In conclusion, sugary drinks are popular because they provide convenience, quick energy, and are heavily promoted through advertising. However, measures such as taxation, education, and improved labeling can help reduce their consumption and encourage healthier lifestyles.

Sugary drinks are now a common part of many people's daily routines, especially among younger generations. Their popularity continues to grow, but there are also concerns about the health problems linked to excessive consumption. This essay examines why these beverages are so appealing and what can be done to reduce their use.
One major reason is the immediate boost of energy they seem to provide. Students often drink sweetened coffee before classes, while office workers may choose energy drinks or soft drinks when they feel tired. Convenience is another important factor. These beverages can be found almost everywhere, from supermarkets to vending machines, making them an easy option. Marketing also has a strong influence. Companies spend large amounts on advertisements and frequently use famous personalities to attract attention, particularly from younger consumers. At the same time, many people are not fully aware of how much sugar these drinks contain or the long-term health risks associated with drinking them regularly.
There are several practical ways to address this issue. Raising the price through additional taxes could discourage frequent purchases. Better labeling on packaging would also help consumers make informed decisions. Another useful approach is education. Public campaigns, school programs, and workplace initiatives could explain the dangers of excessive sugar intake and encourage healthier choices such as water or unsweetened drinks. Limiting the availability of sugary beverages in certain settings may further reduce consumption.
To conclude, the widespread popularity of sugary drinks is driven by convenience, advertising, and the perception that they provide energy. Nevertheless, a combination of education, clearer information, and government policies could help people make healthier decisions and reduce their dependence on these beverages.

In the Third World, children are usually sent to factories for laborious work. Many people believe that it is merely exploitation, while others think it is a good opportunity for them to life experience. In any case, children have their right to live and study in peaceful conditions. Therefore, using them as workforce is considered an unacceptable action.First of all, children are not workers. They have just learned about the vast world and do not have any experience or concept of working. Since these innocent children are naive and , they can be easily cheated and exploited. There are many examples of this in poor nations. Because using children is cheap and to control, many enterprises hire them and don’t pay them much. Although the government in these countries has tried its best, this kind of taking advantage of children cannot be eliminated.Moreover, children do not need such thing as ‘valuable work experience’ that is supposedly ‘important for learning and taking responsibility’. The brief responsibility of children is learning. They are not old enough to understand what working experiences are. Nevertheless, they can help parents do chores or housework. This will be a much better way for them to become more responsible for . In addition, childhood is one of the most remarkable memories and must not be taken away by forcing them to work.In conclusion, since all children are the great concern of parent and society, they should be allowed to enjoy life and rather than to work. Hence, one must ponder what view is actually appropriate for the sake of the children.

In many developing countries, children are employed in factories, farms, and other workplaces from a very young age. While some people argue that working helps children gain practical skills and prepare for adulthood, others believe that child labor is a form of exploitation. In my opinion, children should not be used as a labor force because they deserve access to education, safety, and a proper childhood.
To begin with, children are physically and emotionally immature, making them vulnerable in working environments. They often lack the knowledge and experience needed to protect themselves from unfair treatment. As a result, employers may take advantage of them by paying low wages, assigning dangerous tasks, or requiring them to work long hours. This situation is particularly common in poorer regions where families face financial difficulties and labor regulations are not strictly enforced. Consequently, many children suffer from poor working conditions instead of receiving the care and protection they need.
Another reason child labor should be discouraged is that it interferes with education. The primary responsibility of children is to learn and develop the skills necessary for their future. When they spend most of their time working, they may be forced to miss school or abandon their studies altogether. This limits their opportunities later in life and can trap them in a cycle of poverty. Although some people claim that work teaches responsibility, there are healthier ways for children to learn such values. Helping with household chores, participating in community activities, or taking part in school projects can provide valuable life lessons without sacrificing their education.
Furthermore, childhood is a unique stage of life that should be devoted to growth, learning, and personal development. Children need time to play, form friendships, and explore their interests. Excessive work can place unnecessary pressure on them and deprive them of experiences that are essential for healthy development.
In conclusion, although some people believe child labor offers useful experience, its negative consequences far outweigh any potential benefits. Children should be protected from exploitation and given the opportunity to focus on education and personal growth. Society has a responsibility to ensure that every child can enjoy a safe and meaningful childhood.

In many parts of the developing world, children still work in factories, workshops, and agricultural fields. Some people view this as a way for young people to gain experience and contribute to their families, whereas others see it as a serious violation of children's rights. I believe that children should not be employed as workers because their well-being, education, and development must come first.
One major concern is that children are particularly vulnerable to exploitation. Unlike adults, they may not fully understand their rights or recognize when they are being treated unfairly. This makes it easier for employers to pay them very little, require long working hours, or place them in unsafe conditions. In many low-income countries, economic hardship encourages businesses to hire children because they are a cheaper source of labor. As a result, young workers often bear the burden of difficult jobs while receiving few benefits in return.
Education is another important issue. Childhood should be a period of learning, both inside and outside the classroom. When children spend much of their time working, their studies often suffer. Some may attend school irregularly, while others leave education completely in order to earn money. This can have lasting consequences, reducing their future opportunities and making it harder for them to escape poverty. Responsibility is certainly an important value, but it can be learned through family duties, school activities, and everyday experiences rather than full-time employment.
There is also the question of childhood itself. Growing up is about more than preparing for work. Children need opportunities to play, build friendships, and develop their interests. These experiences contribute to their emotional and social development and cannot easily be replaced once they are lost.
To sum up, although supporters of child labor sometimes argue that it provides practical experience, the disadvantages are far more significant. Children deserve the chance to study, grow, and enjoy their early years in a safe environment. For that reason, societies should focus on protecting children and ensuring that work does not come at the expense of their childhood.

The line graph illustrates the proportion of male and female students graduated from Canadian universities from 1992 to 2007.
Overall, there were 40. 000 more female graduates than male graduates in 1992 and this trend remained constant for the given time period.
In the starting year, in 1992 the rate of the female gender representatives was slight below 100.000, after which line grew up to approximately 105.000 in 1995 and again dropped down to 100.000 between 1997 and 1998. This graph steadily increased in 1998 and peaked at over 148. 000 in 2007.
The male line graph showed a fluctuation. It's started from the rate 70.000 in 1992 and slowly went up to approximately 78. 000 in 1995 and then went down to above 72. 000 in 1998. In 1998 line dropped, and then showed a slight increase and again a decrease to the rate of about 73.000. In 2001 line indicator grew dramatically and reached at the point of 95. 000 in 2007.

The line graph compares the number of international and domestic students graduating from Australian universities between 1995 and 2010.
Overall, the number of graduates increased for both groups over the period. Domestic students consistently accounted for a larger share of graduates, although the gap between the two categories narrowed toward the end of the period.
In 1995, approximately 80,000 domestic students graduated from Australian universities. This figure rose gradually to around 90,000 in 1999 before experiencing a slight decline to 87,000 in 2001. After that, the number increased steadily and reached a peak of roughly 125,000 graduates in 2010.
The number of international graduates was considerably lower at the beginning of the period, standing at about 30,000 in 1995. It climbed slowly to nearly 40,000 by 2000 and then grew more rapidly over the following years. By 2005, the figure had exceeded 55,000 and continued to rise, reaching approximately 85,000 graduates in 2010.
In summary, both domestic and international graduate numbers increased over the 15-year period, with the most significant growth occurring among international students.

The graph shows changes in the number of domestic and international students who graduated from universities in Australia between 1995 and 2010.
Overall, graduation numbers rose for both groups during the period shown. Domestic students remained the larger group throughout, but international graduates increased at a faster pace.
At the start of the period, around 80,000 domestic students completed their university education. The figure grew steadily and reached about 90,000 by 1999. A small decline followed in the early 2000s, when the number slipped to roughly 87,000. After that, the trend reversed, and graduate numbers increased consistently to approximately 125,000 in 2010, the highest point on the graph.
International graduates began at a much lower level of around 30,000 in 1995. Growth was relatively modest during the first few years, with the figure rising to nearly 40,000 by 2000. From then on, the increase became more pronounced. The number surpassed 55,000 in the middle of the decade and eventually reached about 85,000 by the end of the period.
Overall, while both categories experienced growth, the rise in international graduates was particularly notable, reducing the difference between the two groups over time.

Despite the fact that physical punishment of pupils at schools is decreasing, it is considered by many that it improves discipline. This essay completely disagrees with this view because such measures are applied to those who are unable to counterattack and it maybe the easiest way but also the most inhumane.
Firstly, physical assault is usually applied by teachers against children who are not able to stop the violence. In other words, it is not highly probable that an adolescent will try to hurt a teenager who may stop the hitting or even push it back. Thus, kids are at higher risk because the only thing they are able to do in such cases is just try to run away. Moreover, children should understand that they earn what they deserve meaning that the level of repercussions should correspond to the seriousness of so called crime. For example, Sweden schools have publicly available the set of rules and non-physical measures that will be taken against the violators, that confirms that other methods are also effective to keep appropriate kids’ behaviour.
Secondly, it is much more easier for many to maintain a discipline using the fear of corporal punishment. This means that it seems unnecessary and time-waste for grown-ups to try to apply other methods while a hectic kid may be calmed down just in few seconds using the fear of pain. Pupils may not be involved in a lesson and interrupt a teacher because they are not interested in the subject that may be caused by the fact that the teacher was not able to select appropriate technics to convey knowledge. For example, a research conducted by a US institution revealed that 90% of corporal punishments were applied to pupils who did not l listened to their teachers during a lesson.
In conclusion, physical violence should be discontinued in schools because mostly weak kids are in danger and they are subject for such punishments because adolescents chose the simplest way of violence and fear to better the behaviour.

Many people believe that strict discipline is necessary in schools and that harsh punishments can help maintain order among students. However, I completely disagree with this opinion because physical punishment can harm children psychologically and fails to address the real causes of misbehavior.
To begin with, corporal punishment can have serious negative effects on children's mental well-being. Teachers are authority figures, and students are expected to trust and respect them. When physical force is used as a disciplinary tool, children may feel frightened, humiliated, or even develop long-term anxiety. Instead of learning why their behavior was inappropriate, students may simply become afraid of making mistakes. For instance, educational studies have shown that children who experience physical punishment are more likely to suffer from low self-esteem and emotional stress compared with those disciplined through non-violent methods.
Another reason why physical punishment should not be used is that it often ignores the underlying causes of disruptive behavior. Students may misbehave because they are struggling academically, facing personal difficulties, or feeling disengaged in the classroom. Punishing them physically does little to solve these problems. In contrast, effective communication, counseling, and positive reinforcement can help teachers understand students' needs and encourage better behavior. For example, many schools in Finland have successfully reduced disciplinary problems by focusing on student support services rather than punitive measures.
In conclusion, physical punishment is neither an effective nor a humane way to improve discipline in schools. It can damage children's emotional health and fails to address the reasons behind their behavior. Therefore, schools should adopt supportive and constructive approaches to maintaining discipline.

Although some people argue that physical punishment helps schools maintain discipline, I do not believe it is an appropriate solution. In my view, such practices can be harmful to children and are far less effective than alternative methods of dealing with misconduct.
One major concern is the impact on students themselves. A child who is physically punished by a teacher may obey rules out of fear, but that does not necessarily mean they understand the value of good behavior. Being hit or threatened can leave students feeling embarrassed, anxious, or resentful. Since teachers are meant to provide guidance and support, using violence can damage the relationship between educators and learners. Over time, this may create a negative learning environment rather than a productive one.
Physical punishment is also limited because it focuses on the behavior rather than its cause. Students often act out for reasons that are not immediately visible. Some may be struggling with family issues, while others may find lessons difficult or uninteresting. Simply punishing them does not solve these underlying problems. Schools that rely on discussion, mentoring, and behavioral support programs are often more successful in encouraging lasting improvements. These approaches help students understand the consequences of their actions instead of merely fearing punishment.
To conclude, corporal punishment should not be used in schools. It can harm students emotionally and does little to tackle the reasons behind poor behavior. More constructive disciplinary strategies are likely to produce better outcomes for both students and teachers.

Nowadays having a job is a common type of lifestyle for all individuals. As a matter of fact, jobs are very important because is the only way that people have to earn money. However, in many countries, the distribution of salary is not equal and a small number of people earn higher salaries than others.
Some individuals think that this is good for the country because goverment encourage only categories that are more in touch with money, such as employees who work in banks, offices and who has a business. In other words, the economy of the country can increase in a significant way since goverments incentivate these categories by giving them extremely high salaries.
On the other hand, there are those who argue that goverments should not allow salaries above a certain level because there are a lot of employees, such as workers and teachers that have a very small salary. In fact, they are the ones who works for the wealth and the future of the society and it is unfair that they are discourage. Moreover, I also believe that these kind of jobs are more stressful and challenging than others and they work as good as categories that have higher salaries.
In conclusion, I hold the view that everybody should have an equal distribution of salary and it is unfair that goverments incentivate only a certain category of workers. All individuals should be paid according to the commitment they give and we should not think only about the economy of the country.

In modern society, income plays a crucial role in people's lives because it allows them to meet their needs and maintain a reasonable standard of living. However, in many countries, there is a significant difference between the salaries earned by various professions. While some people believe that high salaries should be permitted, others argue that governments should impose limits on earnings. This essay will discuss both views and explain why I believe salary differences should be reduced.
On the one hand, supporters of high salaries argue that they provide motivation for people to work harder and develop valuable skills. Individuals in senior management positions, financial institutions, or successful businesses often take greater responsibilities and contribute significantly to economic growth. As a result, offering them higher salaries can encourage innovation, productivity, and investment. In addition, competitive salaries may attract talented professionals who can help companies and national economies remain successful.
On the other hand, many people believe that extremely high salaries are unfair, especially when essential workers receive much lower pay. Teachers, nurses, and public service employees make important contributions to society, yet they are often rewarded less generously than executives or financial professionals. This situation may create social inequality and reduce motivation among workers who perform valuable but less profitable jobs. Governments should therefore ensure that income differences do not become excessive and that all employees receive fair compensation for their efforts.
In conclusion, although high salaries can encourage ambition and economic development, large income gaps may lead to inequality and dissatisfaction. I believe that governments should take measures to reduce extreme differences in earnings while ensuring that workers are rewarded according to their responsibilities and contributions to society.

Having a job is an essential part of life for most people, as it provides the income needed to support themselves and their families. Nevertheless, earnings vary greatly between professions, and in many countries a relatively small group of people receives salaries that are far higher than those of ordinary workers. While some consider this acceptable, others believe governments should limit such differences.
Those in favor of high salaries often point out that certain positions involve considerable responsibility and risk. Business leaders, investors, and senior managers may make decisions that affect thousands of employees and generate substantial economic value. Higher pay can therefore act as a reward for expertise and hard work, while also encouraging individuals to pursue demanding careers. From this perspective, allowing top performers to earn more can benefit the economy as a whole.
However, there is also a strong argument against excessive income inequality. Many occupations that are vital to society, including teaching and healthcare, are not rewarded at the same level despite their importance. People working in these sectors contribute to education, public welfare, and future generations, yet they often earn far less than those in the corporate world. When salary gaps become too large, they can create a sense of unfairness and weaken social cohesion.
To conclude, higher salaries may be justified in some cases because of greater responsibility and economic contribution. Even so, governments should prevent income disparities from becoming extreme and ensure that all workers receive fair recognition for the value they bring to society.

In many countries the proportion of older people is steadily increasing. What problems will this cause for individuals and society? Suggest some measures that could be taken to reduce the impact of ageing populations.It is true that nowadays people in industrialised nations can expect to live longer than ever before. Although there will undoubtedly be some negative consequences to this trend, societies can take steps to mitigate these potential problems.As people live longer and the populations of developed countries grow older, several related problems can be anticipated. The main issue is that there will obviously be more people of retirement age who will be eligible to receive a pension. The proportion of younger, working adults will be smaller, and governments will therefore receive less money in taxes in relation to the size of the population. In other words, an ageing population will mean a greater tax burden working adults. Further pressures will include a rise in the demand for healthcare, and the adults will increasingly have to look after their elderly relatives.There are several actions that governments could take to solve the problems described above. Firstly, a simple solution would be to increase the retirement age for working adults, perhaps from 65 to 70. Nowadays, people of this age tend to be healthy enough to continue a productive working life. A second measure would be for governments to encourage immigration in order to increase the number of working adults who pay taxes. Finally, money from national budgets will need to be taken from other areas and spent vital healthcare, accommodation and transport facilities for the rising numbers of senior citizens.In conclusion, various measures can be taken to tackle the problems that are certain to arise as the populations of countries grow older.This essay covers the task requirements, however some problems of aging population get only a brief mention. The linking of sentences in the second body paragraph is somewhat ‘mechanical’ and could have been improved. Information sequencing and organisation in paragraphs are done well. The range of vocabulary and the fluency of its use, as well as lack of errors are impressive. Overall, this is a good example of how to get Band 8 without writing a very long essay.

In many nations, the proportion of elderly people is rising steadily as life expectancy increases and birth rates decline. While this demographic trend reflects improvements in healthcare and living standards, it also creates a number of challenges for both individuals and society. Several measures can be implemented to lessen the negative effects of an ageing population.
One significant problem is the growing pressure on public finances. As more people reach retirement age, governments must spend larger amounts on pensions and social welfare programs. At the same time, the proportion of working-age citizens may decline, resulting in a smaller tax base to support these expenditures. This imbalance can place considerable strain on national budgets. In addition, ageing populations increase demand for healthcare services. Older individuals are generally more likely to require medical treatment, long-term care, and support services, which can place extra pressure on healthcare systems and caregivers.
Another issue concerns the impact on individuals and families. Many adults may find themselves responsible for caring for elderly parents while also supporting their own children. This can create financial and emotional stress, particularly when professional care services are expensive or unavailable. Furthermore, labor shortages may emerge in certain sectors as large numbers of experienced workers retire from the workforce.
There are several ways to address these challenges. One practical measure is to gradually raise the retirement age, allowing healthy older adults to remain economically active for longer. Governments could also encourage higher workforce participation by providing flexible employment opportunities for senior citizens. Another solution is to attract skilled immigrants who can help offset labor shortages and contribute to tax revenues. In addition, greater investment in healthcare technology and preventive medicine may reduce long-term healthcare costs and improve quality of life for older people.
In conclusion, ageing populations can create economic, social, and healthcare-related difficulties. However, through policies such as extending working lives, encouraging immigration, and strengthening healthcare systems, governments can reduce the impact of these challenges and maintain social and economic stability.

The number of older people is increasing in many countries due to longer life expectancy and lower birth rates. Although this development is often seen as a sign of social progress, it presents several difficulties for governments, communities, and individuals. Fortunately, a range of measures can help societies adapt to these demographic changes.
One of the most immediate concerns is the financial burden associated with an ageing population. As the number of retirees grows, governments are required to allocate more resources to pensions and social support programs. Meanwhile, a relatively smaller working population contributes taxes, making it more difficult to sustain these benefits over time. Healthcare systems may also come under pressure because older adults typically need more frequent medical care, specialized treatment, and long-term support.
The effects are not limited to public services. Families often face additional responsibilities when elderly relatives require assistance with daily living. Many working adults must balance careers, childcare, and eldercare at the same time, which can lead to stress and financial difficulties. In addition, businesses may struggle to replace experienced employees who leave the workforce, creating shortages in certain occupations.
To reduce these impacts, governments can take several steps. Raising the retirement age is one option, especially as many people remain healthy and active well into later life. Encouraging older adults to continue working on a part-time or flexible basis could also help maintain a larger workforce. Another effective approach is to welcome immigrants who can fill labor-market gaps and contribute to economic growth. At the same time, investing in preventive healthcare and modern care services may reduce future costs while improving the well-being of senior citizens.
In summary, an ageing population brings challenges ranging from increased public spending to workforce shortages and family pressures. Nevertheless, with carefully planned policies and long-term investment, societies can manage these issues successfully and continue to prosper despite demographic change.

In recent years, news reports about crime and violence have become increasingly common in newspapers, on television, and across online platforms. While some people believe that such content should be restricted because it may have negative effects on society, I disagree with this view. In my opinion, reporting criminal activities and violent incidents serves an important social function.
First of all, media coverage of crimes can raise public awareness and discourage unlawful behavior. When people are exposed to real examples of criminal acts and their consequences, they are more likely to understand the risks involved. For instance, reports about drug trafficking, theft, or violent assaults often include information about arrests, punishments, and the damage caused to victims. This can act as a warning to potential offenders and encourage citizens to avoid similar behavior. As a result, public exposure to crime-related news may contribute to crime prevention rather than increase criminal activity.
Another reason why such information should not be restricted is that it helps people stay informed about potential dangers in their communities. Citizens have the right to know about serious incidents that may affect their safety. If the media were prevented from reporting crimes, people might underestimate certain risks and fail to take necessary precautions. Furthermore, transparent reporting can increase accountability by ensuring that criminal cases receive public attention and that authorities respond appropriately.
In conclusion, I believe that criminal activities and violent events should continue to be reported in the media. Such coverage can educate the public about the consequences of crime and provide important information about issues affecting society. Therefore, restricting this type of news would do more harm than good.

Crime and violence are frequently featured in modern media, whether on television, in newspapers, or on news websites. Some people argue that this kind of reporting should be limited because it may produce negative consequences. However, I believe that the public should continue to have access to such information.
One reason is that reports of criminal behavior often highlight the serious consequences that follow illegal actions. When people see real cases involving arrests, imprisonment, or the suffering of victims, they gain a clearer understanding of the costs of crime. This can serve as a deterrent, particularly for young people who might otherwise underestimate the risks involved. News coverage therefore has the potential to educate as well as inform.
Another important point is that an informed public is generally a safer public. Information about crimes allows people to be aware of problems occurring in their neighborhoods and to take appropriate precautions when necessary. If violent incidents were hidden from public view, citizens might develop a false sense of security. In addition, media reporting can draw attention to social issues and encourage governments and law-enforcement agencies to address them more effectively.
To sum up, I do not support restricting reports about crime and violence. Although such news can sometimes be disturbing, it plays an important role in informing society and reminding people of the consequences of unlawful behavior. For these reasons, it should remain available to the public.

However, there are many different ways is which employers can implement to increase theit employees satisfaction in the work environment. In this essay, I will discuss why money incetives can be beneficial, and mention different alternatives.
Rewarding employees with money is one of the best ways to guarantee employees retantion and prevent employees tunrover. This is because most people work to provide better life for them and their families, and extra money can help them achieve that. As a result, companies can increase their employees satisfaction and encourage them turn off job offers. For example, yearly bounses are one of the main incentives to encourage people to work harder. However, companies who do not pay enough bounses can loss many special employees.
While money is considerd as one of the main reasons why employees are satisfied, it can add extra expenses on the companies, which can affect their future success. It is widely accepted that offering flexible working hours can positively impact employee performance. However, there are various other strategies that businesses can adopt to enhance job satisfaction in the workplace. In this essay, I will explore the advantages of flexible schedules and outline alternative methods for motivation.
Providing flexible working arrangements is a highly effective method to ensure employee loyalty and reduce staff turnover. This is primarily because individuals seek a healthy work-life balance to spend more time with their families, and controlling their own schedules helps achieve this goal. Consequently, organizations can boost overall morale and deter workers from seeking employment elsewhere. For instance, allowing remote work on certain days is a primary incentive that motivates staff to remain committed. Conversely, firms that demand strict office attendance may lose talented professionals.
Although flexibility is recognized as a major factor in employee satisfaction, it can sometimes disrupt team communication, potentially hindering company operations. Employers can utilize different approaches to inspire their workforce, such as offering professional development courses or creating a more comfortable office environment, which can significantly improve output. For example, many tech companies provide free meals and recreational areas to ensure their staff feel valued during long working hours.
In conclusion, while flexible working conditions can successfully lower employee turnover, it is not always feasible for every type of business to adopt this model. Nevertheless, other workplace enhancements can be implemented to foster a similarly productive environment.

Giving employees control over their own schedules definitely boosts morale. But it's hardly the only way to build a happy, productive team. While flexible hours have clear benefits, businesses have plenty of other tools at their disposal to keep people motivated.
When companies let people set their own hours or work from home, they usually see a huge drop in staff turnover. Most of us are just trying to juggle our careers with our personal lives, and if a job makes that balancing act easier, we're much more likely to stick around. We're also more likely to ignore recruiters. Just look at the rise of hybrid work—giving someone the freedom to skip the morning commute twice a week builds an incredible amount of loyalty. On the flip side, rigid companies that demand five days a week at a desk often watch their best talent walk out the door.
That said, flexibility isn't a silver bullet. In some industries, scattered schedules can completely derail team communication and slow down projects. When that's the case, managers need to get creative. Investing in a team's future through training programs, or simply making the office a place people actually want to be, often does the trick. You see this a lot in the tech world, where catered lunches and comfortable breakout spaces are used to keep stress low during crunch time.
Ultimately, letting people work on their own terms is a fantastic way to keep them around, but it just doesn't work for every business model. Luckily, companies that require people on-site still have plenty of ways to make their teams feel genuinely valued.

"Work isn't everything" is a common refrain, yet conversations about career advancement, promotions, and the daily hustle seem to be dominating our interactions more than ever. While there are certainly benefits to being career-minded, I believe that obsessing over professional success does significantly more harm than good.
If someone constantly talks about their job, they quickly come across as one-dimensional. This hyper-focused mindset can easily alienate friends and damage a person's social life. Furthermore, fixating entirely on climbing the corporate ladder often breeds intense anxiety. When people fail to reach their own lofty expectations, the disappointment can trigger deep depression. In extreme cases, the desperation to succeed might even push individuals toward unethical, career-ending choices just to get ahead.
That being said, completely ignoring your professional trajectory isn't wise either. It makes sense to periodically assess your skills and look for ways to grow in your field. Given today's hyper-competitive job market and widespread economic uncertainty, it is entirely understandable why people are so focused on securing their livelihoods and discussing their next career moves.
Ultimately, while letting career ambitions consume your every waking thought is unhealthy, having open conversations about work can actually be quite constructive. Sharing advice, networking, and discussing goals can help people navigate their industries and build more stable, fulfilling lives.

It’s hard to imagine getting through a single day without a smartphone. These devices have completely embedded themselves into our routines, giving us instant access to social media, navigation, and an endless stream of apps. Given how much they simplify our lives—whether that means instantly pulling up directions to a new restaurant or video chatting with someone across the globe—it’s no wonder people are excited to see where mobile technology goes next.

Voice notes have quickly become a go-to alternative to traditional phone calls and long text messages. This shift makes perfect sense given how much we value multitasking today; leaving a quick audio clip lets people share their thoughts on the fly without having to stop and type or schedule a live conversation. While this trend certainly fits our fast-paced lifestyles, relying heavily on voice messages comes with both distinct benefits and a few noticeable drawbacks.
On the plus side, recording a memo requires practically zero effort compared to typing out a lengthy paragraph. You can just talk naturally, which usually means you can explain a complex situation much faster. Beyond just saving time, audio captures tone of voice and emotion in a way that plain text simply cannot. Hearing someone's actual voice helps convey their mood, making the exchange feel much more personal and connected than a standard chat thread.
However, the convenience for the sender often becomes a burden for the receiver. Unlike a text that can be glanced at anywhere, a voice message demands a quiet environment or a pair of headphones. In meetings or noisy public spaces, listening to an audio clip is basically impossible. Furthermore, people tend to ramble when they speak. A point that could have been a concise sentence often gets buried under pauses and tangents. If the listener is in a rush, digging through a two-minute recording to find one specific detail is incredibly frustrating.
Ultimately, swapping texts for voice messages is a double-edged sword. Whether it is a helpful tool or a minor annoyance really depends on the context of the conversation and the environment of the people involved.

The provided table tracks coffee export volumes—measured in millions of tons—across five continents between 2000 and 2015, recorded at five-year intervals. South America consistently dominated the market, while Oceania exported the smallest share of the group.
The sheer volume of global exports saw a massive jump, rising from 550.5 million tons in 2000 to an impressive 810.2 million by 2015. South America was the primary driver of this growth. Elsewhere, Africa and Asia both enjoyed steady increases in their export capacity. African shipments grew by 15 million tons overall, while Asian output actually more than doubled by the end of 2015. While group study sessions are fantastic for expanding your academic knowledge, hitting the books on your own is just as crucial for developing essential study habits. Both approaches play a major role in a student's success. It is worth exploring, however, which method actually provides the most long-term value for a student's educational growth.
Collaborating with peers is incredibly helpful for deepening our understanding of a complex subject. Industrial pollution is one of the most pressing environmental crises we face today, and many governments have tried to fix it by slapping heavy taxes on factories that dump waste. While the intention is good, I honestly don't think this approach works. In reality, very little has actually changed since these financial penalties were introduced, mostly because it remains easier for companies to simply pay the fees and carry on.
Despite the new taxes, factories haven't stopped relying on cheap, harmful disposal methods. We constantly hear about green alternatives, but the infrastructure just isn't there for the average manufacturing plant to make the switch affordably. Smaller and mid-sized businesses simply don't have the profit margins to overhaul their entire operation without risking bankruptcy. Upgrading to a high-tech water filtration system, for example, requires an enormous amount of physical space and operational downtime that most facilities can't spare. Because of this, managers usually decide that taking the financial hit from a pollution tax is the safer, more practical business choice.
Furthermore, global demand for cheap consumer goods is higher than ever. With a rapidly growing population, factories are under immense pressure to churn out products at breakneck speeds. While a handful of high-profile brands might be able to pivot to completely sustainable manufacturing, the vast majority of the industrial sector doesn't have that luxury. They prefer to pay the fines and keep their assembly lines moving without interruption.
Ultimately, while taxing industrial waste might have seemed like a strong deterrent on paper, it hasn't actually put a dent in global pollution levels. It turns out that when pushed, most corporations are more than willing to eat the cost of a fine if it means protecting their bottom line and maintaining their convenience.

Some people claim that spatial navigation skills are still beneficial in spite of the appearance of applications providing GPS navigation in real time. Modern software is able to give quite accurate directions to different places, and it seems that it will soon replace physical maps and will get rid of the necessity to learn geography. However, in my opinion, there are still some essential advantages to knowing how to navigate independently.
First of all, the physical environment is such a complicated and constantly changing system that it is impossible to automate the navigation process to keep all details. For instance, if a road is closed due to sudden construction, it will unlikely be recognized by GPS immediately. Thus, it is too hard to teach a device to understand all the local changes that you might encounter.
Another important aspect of learning navigation is knowing your surroundings. Spatial awareness is a great source for understanding the layout of a city and its neighborhoods. If you use online maps, you will never know the actual geography or landmarks that contain historical value.
Moreover, learning spatial navigation is a tool of training your brain. It helps to develop memory and spatial intelligence. For instance, people didn't refuse studying math after the calculation device had been invented, because it is still important to understand how it works and it is necessary for developing other sciences.
Finally, there is a lot of places and occasions when you do not have a battery charge or internet access. Then digital maps are not available. But if you need to find your way being in a remote area, it is better to know at least some basic orientation skills. Otherwise, you can occur in an unpleasant situation.
To sum up, I definitely agree with the statement that learning navigation skills is essential in spite of technology progress. Although GPS is able to direct you anywhere you want, it is crucial to continue learning to navigate in order to understand your surroundings, to know your city and understand its peculiarities and, of course, to make your brain work.

Even with GPS and real-time navigation apps on every smartphone, many argue that basic map-reading and spatial skills are still incredibly valuable. It is easy to assume that digital maps will completely erase our need to understand geography, given how accurately they provide turn-by-turn directions. Still, I believe relying entirely on a screen is a mistake, and knowing how to navigate on your own offers some major advantages. 
For one thing, the physical world is messy and unpredictable. A GPS app might not immediately register a sudden road closure, a flooded street, or a newly built detour. If you lack a basic mental map of an area, you are completely helpless the moment the software fails to adapt to real-world conditions. Beyond just avoiding traffic, navigating by yourself actually connects you to your environment. Constantly staring at a blue dot on a screen means you miss out on the actual layout of a city. Learning to orient yourself helps you understand how neighborhoods connect and allows you to discover landmarks you would otherwise just drive past.
Building spatial awareness is also a fantastic way to keep your mind sharp. Forcing yourself to remember routes and visualize directions actively develops your memory. Think about it like basic math: we didn't stop teaching addition and subtraction just because calculators were invented. Understanding the underlying logic of where you are is still crucial.
There is also the obvious issue of technology simply failing. Batteries die, and remote areas frequently lack cell service. If you are hiking or driving through the mountains and lose your signal, knowing how to read a physical map or orient yourself isn't just an outdated hobby—it can keep you out of a genuinely dangerous situation.
Ultimately, while navigation apps are undeniably convenient, they shouldn't entirely replace our natural sense of direction. Developing spatial awareness keeps our brains active, helps us truly experience the places we travel, and ensures we are never left stranded just because a phone died.

The past twenty years have seen a dramatic change in the number of employees who work from home. In this essay, I will explore the advantages and disadvantages of this trend.
On the one hand, both employees and their companies can benefit from this trend. First, workers can choose more comfortable environments for themselves because there are more flexible options. The office settings and daily routines are quite different. Secondly, people can save their commuting time, they can stay with their family when they are in their own house, and they quickly improve their work-life balance. Thirdly, it helps to promote the productivity and efficiency of the business. These employees manage their schedules independently. Workers can get better focus on their tasks. Managers in the company will get more completed projects without office distractions. Similarly, the businesses also can get reduced costs regarding office space.
On the other hand, there are disadvantages too. Firstly, it costs people a lot of money to set up a home office. Many individuals have to spend their savings to buy computers and equipment to support their work at home. Secondly, some workers cannot stand the big isolation from their colleagues, they lose their motivation and quit their jobs.
To conclude, given the fact that working from home helps employees with their daily routines and promotes the productivity of companies, even if there are some drawbacks, I think the benefits outweigh the problems.

Over the last couple of decades, the shift toward remote work has completely transformed the traditional nine-to-five routine. While ditching the daily commute sounds like a dream to many, working from home comes with its own unique set of pros and cons that are worth exploring.
There are definitely clear benefits for both staff and employers. For one thing, people finally have the freedom to design a workspace that actually suits them, rather than being stuck in a rigid corporate cubicle. Without the stress of rush hour traffic, employees gain hours back every single week. This extra time at home naturally leads to a much healthier work-life balance. Beyond personal well-being, businesses often see a massive boost in overall productivity. When people can manage their own schedules and block out office distractions, they tend to get a lot more done. Plus, companies can save a fortune on commercial real estate when half their workforce is logging in from their living rooms.
That being said, the remote lifestyle isn't perfect. Setting up a proper home office isn't cheap, and many workers find themselves spending a lot of money out of pocket just to buy reliable Wi-Fi routers, ergonomic chairs, and extra monitors. Furthermore, not everyone handles isolation well. Being cut off from the casual, day-to-day banter with coworkers can leave some people feeling completely disconnected, which sometimes leads to burnout or even quitting altogether.
Ultimately, while working remotely requires a solid initial setup and can occasionally get lonely, I believe the advantages far outweigh the downsides. The flexibility it provides makes for happier employees and more efficient businesses.

In the diagram, the steps needed to obtain a dough for a pizza are shown, figures are given for grams and time required for the process.
In the initial stage, the staple ingredients, namely, 4 coup of 00 flour, 325 gr of water ( roughly one and a half cups) and a half teaspoon of dry active yeast respectively, are blended together in a cup.
After being placed in a stand mixer, the primordial dough undergo through 3 mixing stages, with the first and the third at slow speed for 2 minutes each, and the second at medium speed for 5 minutes.
Subsequently, the dough is going to be left to rest as long as its thickness has increased twofold, which it would takes at least 2 Hours.
Having gotten rid of the bubbles by squeezing them manually, the resulting portion of dough is either divided thoroughly into 3 slices equally sized or making each of the three parts weigh 275 grams.

In the diagram, the steps needed to obtain fresh pasta dough are illustrated, and figures are provided for the measurements and time required for the procedure.
In the initial stage, the main ingredients, namely, 2 cups of semolina flour, 3 large eggs, and a half teaspoon of olive oil respectively, are combined together in a bowl.
After being transferred to a wooden board, the initial mixture undergoes through 2 kneading stages, with the first at a gentle pace for 5 minutes, and the second at a vigorous pace for 10 minutes.
Subsequently, the dough is going to be left to rest wrapped in plastic film to allow the gluten to relax, which it would take at least 30 minutes.
Having rolled out the dough by pressing it through a machine, the resulting sheet of pasta is either cut thoroughly into thin strands equally sized or making the entire piece into wide ribbons for pappardelle.

The provided diagram breaks down the process of making fresh pasta dough, including the required ingredient measurements and estimated preparation times.
To start, you combine the base ingredients in a large bowl: two cups of semolina flour, three whole eggs, and about half a teaspoon of olive oil. Once the ingredients are fully mixed, the dough is moved to a wooden board for kneading. This hands-on process happens in two distinct phases. First, you knead the mixture gently for five minutes to bring everything together. After that, you work the dough much more vigorously for another ten minutes until it becomes smooth and elastic.
Next, the dough needs some time to rest. It is wrapped tightly in plastic film and left to sit for at least half an hour, which gives the gluten enough time to relax. Finally, the rested dough is flattened into a thin sheet using a pasta machine. From there, you can either slice the sheet into thin, evenly sized strands for spaghetti, or cut the whole piece into much wider ribbons to make pappardelle.

The diagram shows the process of coffee from production to preparing for sale in supermarkets and shops.
Overall, it is certain that coffee goes through 11 different stages from picking the beans to packing in jars.
Firstly, coffee beans are picked and then dried by humans. The dried beans are later roasted in a machine then in the blast freezer machine, it's cooled down rapidly. Furthermore, after cooling out they go through a grinding process where they turned into powder, then mixed with high-temperature water after a while, it goes from straining process.
We are now arriving at the later process where coffee is now frozen in the refrigerator. In the third last stage, again it goes from the grinding process but this time it is frozen and after this, they put them in a vacuum machine where it evaporates the water from coffee to make them dry.
Lastly, the coffee is ready to pack in the jar and ready to sell on the market.

The diagram shows the process of tea from production to preparing for sale in supermarkets and shops.
Overall, it is certain that tea goes through 10 different stages from picking the leaves to packing in boxes.
Firstly, tea leaves are picked and then withered by humans. The withered leaves are later rolled in a machine then in the oxidation room, it's fermented slowly. Furthermore, after fermenting they go through a firing process where they turned into dry flakes, then sorted with high-tech equipment after a while, it goes from grading process.
We are now arriving at the later process where tea is now blended in the factory. In the third last stage, again it goes from the sorting process but this time it is flavored and after this, they put them in a packaging machine where it weighs the portions of tea to make them precise.
Lastly, the tea is ready to pack in the box and ready to sell on the market.

The provided diagram illustrates the step-by-step process of producing tea, from the initial harvesting of the leaves to the final retail packaging. Overall, the production involves ten distinct stages, beginning with hand-picking and ending with the finished product boxed up for consumers.
The process begins when fresh tea leaves are manually harvested and laid out to wither. Once the leaves have lost a sufficient amount of moisture, they are fed into a rolling machine. This prepares them for the oxidation room, where they are left to slowly ferment. Following fermentation, the leaves undergo a high-heat firing process that completely dries them out, turning them into crisp flakes. Afterward, specialized equipment is used to sort and grade the tea based on size and quality.
In the final stages of production, the graded leaves are blended. Depending on the specific product line, they may also be flavored at this point. Next, the tea is routed into an automated packaging machine that precisely weighs out the required portions. Finally, the processed tea is sealed into boxes and distributed to supermarkets for sale.

The provided charts compare the average monthly temperatures and rainfall frequency for Sydney and Melbourne over the course of a year. Overall, while both cities follow the same general seasonal cycle, Melbourne stays noticeably cooler than Sydney year-round. Sydney also tends to experience more rainy days, though the two cities see their wettest weather at completely different times of the year.
Looking at the temperature data, Melbourne's climate is much more variable. Its average daily maximum drops sharply from a summer peak of 26 degrees in January down to just 13 degrees by July. Sydney, on the other hand, enjoys much milder seasonal shifts. Its warmest days average around 28 degrees in February, and the city only cools down to about 18 degrees mid-year. During the dead of winter—specifically July and August—Melbourne's overnight lows routinely dip to a chilly 6 degrees.
Rainfall trends also move in opposite directions. In Sydney, rainy days are most frequent in early autumn, peaking at about 15 days in March before drying out to around 8 days per month throughout the spring. Melbourne sees the reverse pattern. Its late summers are relatively dry, with just 8 rainy days in February, but the city gets steadily wetter as the year progresses, averaging 14 rainy days a month from July through September.
Ultimately, Melbourne is characterized by its cold, damp winters, whereas Sydney sees its heaviest rainfall while the weather is still warm.

In my opinion, allowing children to idolize celebrities can often be detrimental, as they frequently set a poor example.Many of today’s celebrities tend to lack moral values, and they promote behaviour inappropriate for young people. For instance, some music videos feature explicit lyrics glorifying drug use and violence. In addition, the media often reports on celebrities who indulge in excessive partying or drinking alcohol despite being underage. These types of headlines can have a negative influence on impressionable teens who might falsely believe that they should follow suit to appear cool or successful.Poor morals aren’t the only issue, as many popular stars are also well-known for their irresponsible spending habits. They routinely splurge on luxury items such as designer clothing or expensive cars without any consideration for budgeting or saving money responsibly. This kind of behaviour is dangerous because it is normalizing materialism and teaching teens that costly possessions will bring them happiness instead of focusing on what really matters in life: meaningful relationships, knowledge, and hard work.To conclude, young adults should keep in mind that society’s perception of fame and fortune is based on temporary, insubstantial things rather than what actually matters. In the grand scheme of things, it would be far more beneficial for teenagers to spend time developing their own set of values rooted in quality education and strong family relationships.

It is not uncommon for teenagers to follow social media influencers. From daily vloggers to fashion models, many young people around the world look up to these internet personalities and attempt to emulate their lifestyles. In my opinion, allowing children to idolize influencers can often be detrimental, as they frequently set unrealistic expectations.
Many of today’s social media stars tend to present heavily edited versions of reality, and they promote standards that are unattainable for young people. For instance, some popular creators use digital filters to showcase flawless appearances and luxurious vacations. In addition, the internet often rewards influencers who engage in dramatic controversies or dangerous pranks to gain views. These types of content can have a negative influence on impressionable teens who might falsely believe that they should act similarly to gain attention or social validation.
Unrealistic lifestyles aren’t the only issue, as many internet personalities are also well-known for their relentless promotion of consumerism. They routinely advertise expensive beauty products or trendy fashion items without any consideration for the financial burden it places on their audience. This kind of behaviour is dangerous because it is normalizing hyper-consumerism and teaching teens that purchasing advertised goods will bring them happiness instead of focusing on what really matters in life: genuine experiences, personal growth, and hard work.
To conclude, young adults should keep in mind that the internet's perception of success and beauty is based on curated, superficial things rather than what actually matters. In the grand scheme of things, it would be far more beneficial for teenagers to spend time developing their own self-esteem rooted in real-world achievements and authentic friendships.

It seems like almost every teenager today follows at least a few social media influencers. Whether they are watching daily vloggers or fashion icons, kids everywhere are glued to these online personalities, often trying to copy how they dress and live. But honestly, I think treating influencers like role models does significantly more harm than good, primarily because they are selling a reality that simply doesn't exist.
The main issue is that these internet stars curate heavily edited versions of their lives, pushing lifestyle standards that are entirely out of reach for the average kid. A quick scroll through a feed usually turns up endless photos tweaked with digital filters, showing off flawless skin and non-stop, expensive vacations. On top of that, social media platforms tend to reward creators who pull dangerous stunts or stir up petty drama just to get clicks. For an impressionable teenager, consuming this content daily can be incredibly damaging. They can easily fall into the trap of thinking they need to act out or look physically perfect just to be liked or noticed by their peers.
Beyond the fake lifestyles, there is also the relentless pressure to buy things. Influencers are practically walking billboards, constantly pushing their followers to purchase the latest skincare routine or fast-fashion haul without caring if these items are actually affordable for their young audience. This endless cycle of sponsored posts is dangerous. It normalizes hyper-consumerism and convinces kids that buying the right products is the fast track to happiness, pulling their attention away from things that actually matter like personal growth and real-world experiences.
Ultimately, teenagers need to understand that the glossy success they see online is highly curated and entirely superficial. Rather than trying to emulate a stranger's digital highlight reel, kids would be much better off focusing on their actual lives—building authentic friendships, working hard, and developing genuine self-confidence.

This bar chart illustrates how global energy consumption is distributed among four major nations across three distinct years: 1980, 2010, and a projected outlook for 2040. Right away, it is obvious that the United States and China dominate global energy use, taking up the vast majority of the share across all timeframes. Meanwhile, Russia and Germany account for significantly smaller fractions, with both showing a downward trend following 1980.
When looking closely at the data, China's energy footprint shows rapid, uninterrupted growth. The country accounted for just 10% of global consumption in 1980, doubled that figure to roughly 20% by 2010, and is projected to hit nearly 25% by 2040. The United States tells a completely different story. While it was easily the biggest energy consumer historically—claiming over a quarter of the global total in 1980—its share shrank to 22% by 2010. Forecasters expect this decline to continue, projecting a further drop to roughly 18% by 2040.
As for the smaller consumers, Germany consistently held the lowest share of the group. Starting at about 6% in 1980, its slice of the pie dipped slightly by 2010 and is expected to shrink to a mere 2% by 2040. Russia experienced a similar, albeit less drastic, reduction. After dropping to a 10% share in 2010, its proportion is expected to plateau and remain at that exact same level through the 2040 projections.
